Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Nov-20-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
Luis A. Serrano, Esq. QUILL & ARROW, LLP (310) 933-4271 lserrano@quillarrowlaw.com Attorneys for Plaintiff, VAN HOANG NGO
Madison King, Esq., THETA LAW FIRM, LLP (424) 297-3103 eservice@thetafirm.com, for Defendant Mercedes-Benz USA, LLC.

Hearing held. Not ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT MERCEDES-BENZ OF STEVENS CREEK Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration

Defendant Mercedes-Benz USA, LLC's motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is denied.

Plaintiff Van Hoang Ngo purchased a vehicle from a retailer in July 2022, executing a contract which included an agreement to arbitrate. Ngo filed a complaint in July 2023 alleging violations of the Song-Beverly Act (breaches of express and implied warranty) and negligent repair. MBUSA now files this motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings, alleging that it has standing to bring the motion on the grounds of equitable estoppel and its status as a third-party beneficiary of the arbitration agreement.

The court rejects MBUSA's equitable estoppel argument because Ngo's claims are not '"intimately founded in and intertwined' with the underlying contract obligations." (Ochoa v. Ford Motor Company (2023) 89 Cal.App.5th 1324, 1333; Kielar v. Superior Court (2023) 94 Cal.App.5th 614, 620; Montemayor v. Ford Motor Co. (2023) 92 Cal.App.5th 958, 971 (all disagreeing with Felisilda v. FCA US LLC (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 486).) Ngo's complaint does not reference or rely on the financing agreement. Ngo is trying to enforce MBUSA's statutory obligations under the Song-Beverly Act to adequately repair, replace, or repurchase the car at issue.

Additionally, MBUSA is not a third-party beneficiary of the arbitration agreement. MBUSA must show (1) "the third party would in fact benefit from the contract"; (2) "a motivating purpose of the contracting parties was to provide a benefit to the third party"; and (3) permitting the third party to enforce the contract "is consistent with the objectives of the contract and the reasonable expectations of the contracting parties." (Goonewardene v. ADP, LLC (2019) 6 Cal.5th 817, 830.) The contract does not demonstrate an express intent to benefit MBUSA, nor would it be a reasonable expectation of the contracting parties that MBUSA would enforce the arbitration clause.

Because MBUSA does not have standing to bring the motion to compel arbitration, the motion is denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer: W. Trupek, Not reported (302/RBU)
